docs.title: Weekly Digest
docs.text:
Every week, JarvisForResearchers synthesises the most notable robotics & AI papers published in the last seven days into a single post — one overview, per-paper highlights, and cross-cutting trends.

New digests appear here automatically after each --digest pipeline run.

docs.title: This Week in Robotics — 2026, Week 17
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-05-01

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This week's submissions highlight advancements across perception, world modeling, and real-time interaction, demonstrating a trend toward unified, feed-forward systems. OmniRobotHome addresses the complexity of real-time, multiadic interaction in unstructured home settings, showing that wide-area sensing and behavioral modeling improve safety metrics. In the domain of autonomous driving, HERMES++ tackles the integration challenge by unifying 3D scene understanding and future geometry prediction within a BEV framework, enhanced by LLM queries. Complementing this, GenWildSplat pushes the boundaries of 3D reconstruction by offering a feed-forward solution for sparse, unconstrained images, bypassing the computational overhead of per-scene optimization. A cross-cutting theme is the move away from iterative optimization toward integrated, single-pass inference pipelines for robustness and speed.

docs.title: OmniRobotHome: A Multi-Camera Platform for Real-Time Multiadic Human-Robot Interaction
docs.text:
OmniRobotHome is a room-scale platform integrating 48 hardware-synchronized RGB cameras and two Franka arms to enable real-time, occlusion-robust multiadic human-robot interaction in a natural home environment. The system employs an end-to-end real-time sensing pipeline, achieving an Avg. Cycle time of 63.12s with a Dynamic Policy compared to a Non-aware baseline. Furthermore, the Dynamic Policy achieved 28.5 Human Hits, indicating improved interaction robustness.

Why it matters: For complex, multi-agent tasks in unstructured environments, real-time, room-scale 3D perception is the critical enabler.

docs.title: HERMES++: Toward a Unified Driving World Model for 3D Scene Understanding and Generation
docs.text:
HERMES++ is a unified driving world model that integrates 3D scene understanding and future geometry prediction within a single framework by leveraging a Bird’s-Eye View (BEV) representation and LLM-enhanced world queries. The framework utilizes a Joint Geometric Optimization strategy to enforce structural integrity, resulting in an 8.2% reduction in 3s point cloud generation error compared to DriveX [18]. It also shows a 9.2% outperformance on the CIDEr metric for understanding tasks versus Omni-Q [15].

Why it matters: Unified world models are necessary to bridge the gap between semantic interpretation (LLMs) and physical simulation (geometry prediction).

docs.title: Generalizable Sparse-View 3D Reconstruction from Unconstrained Images
docs.text:
GenWildSplat is a feed-forward framework that achieves generalizable 3D scene reconstruction from sparse, unposed internet images by integrating appearance adaptation and occlusion modeling without requiring per-scene optimization. The framework achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward rendering quality while maintaining an Inference Time of 3 seconds. This is accomplished by using an appearance adapter modulated by a light code estimated by a light encoder.

Why it matters: For real-time 3D reconstruction from unconstrained images, feed-forward models that avoid per-scene optimization are superior to optimization-based methods.

docs.title: Trends & Observations
docs.text:
• There is a clear architectural trend toward unified models (HERMES++) that merge disparate capabilities, such as semantic understanding and geometric prediction, into a single framework.
  • Feed-forward inference is emerging as a preferred paradigm for perception tasks (GenWildSplat), offering significant speed advantages over traditional optimization-based methods.
  • The integration of large language models (LLMs) is being leveraged not just for high-level reasoning, but to directly guide physical simulation and scene evolution (HERMES++).
  • Robustness in complex environments is being addressed through comprehensive sensing (OmniRobotHome) and explicit modeling of physical constraints (HERMES++).
  • Decoupling appearance from geometry (GenWildSplat) allows for controllable rendering under novel environmental conditions, such as different lighting.

docs.title: This Week in Robotics — 2026, Week 20
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-05-24

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This week's research shows a strong focus on refining the fidelity and interpretability of complex generative and predictive models across vision and robotics. In self-supervised learning, V-JEPA 2.1 demonstrates that applying Dense Predictive Loss across all tokens, coupled with Deep Self-Supervision, yields measurable gains in anticipation tasks. On the generation side, REFDECODER addresses detail loss in video VAEs by explicitly conditioning the decoder on high-fidelity reference signals, achieving PSNR improvements over existing methods. Furthermore, the field is grappling with the limitations of learned metrics; SysCON3D provides a necessary framework to test 3D consistency evaluators against geometric hallucinations. Finally, several papers tackle specific bottlenecks: CARV optimizes variance reduction in diffusion sampling, ConvexTok offers a rigorous optimization approach to tokenization, and MODIRECT targets the directional motion blindness in Video-LLMs.

docs.title: V-JEPA 2.1: Unlocking Dense Features in Video Self-Supervised Learning
docs.text:
V-JEPA 2.1 enhances self-supervised learning for dense visual understanding in images and videos by applying a Dense Predictive Loss across all tokens and incorporating Deep Self-Supervision. The method introduces a Dense Predictive Loss applied to both masked and unmasked tokens, and incorporates Deep Self-Supervision by applying the loss hierarchically across multiple intermediate encoder layers. This resulted in an mAP of 7.71 on Ego4D for short-term object-interaction anticipation.

Why it matters: To improve dense feature quality in SSL, extending the predictive loss to cover all tokens (masked and unmasked) is crucial.

docs.title: RefDecoder: Enhancing Visual Generation with Conditional Video Decoding
docs.text:
REFDECODER enhances visual generation by introducing a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder that injects high-fidelity reference image signals directly into the decoding process via reference attention, mitigating detail loss and inconsistency inherent in unconditional VAE decoders. REFDECODER uses reference attention to inject high-fidelity reference image signals into the decoder, and employs a latent token dropout strategy to force the decoder to recover spatial details from reference tokens. This approach yielded a +2.1 dB PSNR improvement on WebVid compared to VideoVAE+.

Why it matters: REFDECODER is a drop-in replacement for existing video VAE decoders, requiring no retraining of the diffusion model or freezing the encoder.

docs.title: Can These Views Be One Scene? Evaluating Multiview 3D Consistency when 3D Foundation Models Hallucinate
docs.text:
This paper introduces SysCON3D, a controlled benchmark, and a parametric family of neural metrics to diagnose and improve the robustness of ground-truth-free multiview 3D consistency evaluators against geometric hallucinations. The authors developed SysCON3D, a controlled benchmark, and a parametric neural metric family that decomposes evaluators into backbone, residual, and aggregation components. This led to MASt3R-W-IMQ being up to 3\(\times\) more robust than MEt3R.

Why it matters: When evaluating 3D consistency without ground truth, be aware that learned metrics inherit the failure modes of their underlying reconstruction backbones.

docs.title: Variance Reduction for Expectations with Diffusion Teachers
docs.text:
CARV is a compute-aware variance-accounting framework that introduces a hierarchical Monte Carlo estimator by amortizing expensive upstream computations over cheap diffusion-noise resamples, sharpened by timestep importance sampling and stratified-inverse-CDF construction. CARV implements a hierarchical Monte Carlo estimator by caching expensive upstream computations and resampling cheap diffusion noise. This framework achieved Effective Compute Multipliers (ECM) of 2-3\(\times\) compared to uniform-IID sampling.

Why it matters: When upstream computation is expensive relative to denoising cost (\(c_{render+encode} \gg c_{denoise}\)), prioritize compute reuse by caching expensive states.

docs.title: Tokenisation via Convex Relaxations
docs.text:
ConvexTok formulates tokeniser construction as a linear program relaxation of an NP-hard integer program, allowing for the computation of near-optimal tokenisers and providing lower bounds on compression. The paper formulates tokeniser construction as a linear program (LP) derived from an equivalent integer program (IP), and proposes three rounding schemes to discretize the LP solution. The resulting tokenisers consistently outperformed other tokenisers on intrinsic metrics.

Why it matters: ConvexTok offers a mathematically rigorous way to approach tokeniser design by framing it as an optimization problem.

docs.title: Explicit Stair Geometry Conditioning for Robust Humanoid Locomotion
docs.text:
This paper introduces an explicit stair geometry conditioning framework that uses interpretable parameters like step height, step depth, and current yaw angle to condition a PPO-based locomotion policy for robust humanoid stair climbing. The framework conditions a PPO-based policy directly on step height, step depth, and current yaw, enabling anticipatory gait modulation. The policy demonstrated an MAE(\(h_{step}\)) of 0.6 cm across unseen stair geometries in simulation.

Why it matters: Explicitly parameterizing terrain geometry (step height, step depth) provides more structured and task-relevant information than implicit latent representations for locomotion.

docs.title: Which Way Did It Move? Diagnosing and Overcoming Directional Motion Blindness in Video-LLMs
docs.text:
The paper introduces MODIRECT and DeltaDirect to diagnose and overcome directional motion blindness in Video-LLMs, which stems from a direction binding gap where motion signals are present but not reliably linked to verbal answers. The authors introduced DeltaDirect, a training-only projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. This resulted in a motion direction accuracy of 85.4% on MODIRECT-SYNBENCH, up from 25.9% for the vanilla baseline.

Why it matters: Training objectives like DeltaDirect can strengthen low-level motion cues at the visual-language interface, improving performance.

docs.title: Trends & Observations
docs.text:
• There is a clear trend toward making learned metrics more robust by moving beyond simple aggregation, as seen in the development of parametric neural metrics in SysCON3D.
  • Several papers address the efficiency of deep learning pipelines, whether through compute amortization (CARV) or by providing mathematically grounded design choices (ConvexTok).
  • The integration of explicit, interpretable geometric parameters (e.g., step height, step depth) is proving more effective for control tasks than relying solely on implicit latent representations.
  • Video understanding research is focusing on fine-grained signal binding, specifically addressing directional motion blindness in Video-LLMs (MODIRECT).
  • Self-supervised learning advancements (V-JEPA 2.1) are pushing the boundary of feature density by modifying the loss function application across all tokens.

docs.title: This Week in Robotics — 2026, Week 21
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-05-31

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This week's papers span diverse domains, from economic modeling to embodied AI and scientific software engineering, revealing consistent themes around structural rigor and controlled optimization. In economics, "Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials" moves beyond standard regression by enforcing analytical tractability through a demand-first formulation. For robotics, "Latent Action Diffusion for Cross-Embodiment Manipulation" addresses the challenge of skill transfer across heterogeneous hardware by aligning action spaces in a latent manifold. A parallel theme of systematic refinement appears in "SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills," which optimizes procedural knowledge externally without altering core model weights. Furthermore, the reliability of AI systems is scrutinized in two ways: "TriSplat" achieves simulation readiness by natively representing geometry with oriented triangles, while "Physics Is All You Need?" emphasizes that rigorous human supervision dictates the trustworthiness of scientific AI outputs.

docs.title: Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials
docs.text: The Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN) is a demand-first neural model that represents log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing elasticities to be derived exactly as derivatives of the learned demand surface. ICDN decomposes price responses into linear own-price effects, directed cross-price interactions, and spline-based nonlinear refinements. It computes own- and cross-price elasticities by exploiting analytic derivatives of product-specific spline bases, avoiding dense Jacobian or Hessian evaluations. Why it matters: ICDN provides a framework to derive elasticities exactly from a single, smooth demand surface, enhancing analytical tractability.

docs.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
docs.text: SkillOpt introduces a systematic, controllable text-space optimizer that treats agent skills as an external state, allowing for deep-learning-style optimization of procedural knowledge without modifying the frozen target model weights. The framework uses rollout batches, reflection minibatches, and textual learning rates to optimize skills. Across its empirical study, SkillOpt achieved an average gain of +23.5 points over no skill when using GPT-5.5 in a direct chat setting. Why it matters: SkillOpt provides a framework to systematically optimize agent procedures (skills) using deep-learning optimization principles without requiring access to model weights.

docs.title: TriSplat: Simulation-Ready Feed-Forward 3D Scene Reconstruction
docs.text: TriSplat is a feed-forward 3D scene reconstruction network that uses oriented triangle primitives to directly export simulation-ready mesh scenes from sparse, unposed images in a single forward pass. The network jointly predicts geometry, appearance, and camera poses using oriented triangle primitives. This triangle-native representation eliminates post-hoc mesh extraction, allowing direct consumption by physics engines. Why it matters: For simulation-ready 3D reconstruction, using explicit surface primitives (like triangles) natively in a feed-forward pipeline avoids costly post-processing steps.

docs.title: Latent Action Diffusion for Cross-Embodiment Manipulation
docs.text: This paper introduces Latent Action Diffusion to unify diverse end-effector action spaces into a semantically aligned latent space, enabling cross-embodiment policy learning for multi-robot control and skill transfer. The method uses contrastive learning to unify diverse action spaces into a single latent space. Factorizing policies into a latent, embodiment-agnostic policy and embodiment-specific action decoders resulted in up to a 25.3% improvement in manipulation success rate over single-embodiment policies. Why it matters: Use contrastive learning to bridge heterogeneous action spaces when transferring skills between robots with different end-effectors.

docs.title: Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring
docs.text: This study investigates algorithmic monoculture in hiring by analyzing a novel dataset from the vendor pymetrics, finding clear racial disparities and systemic rejection patterns when examining hiring decisions on a per-position basis. The research provided evidence of systemic rejections at scale due to deployed hiring algorithms. Specifically, the adverse impact against Black applicants per position was measured at 10.62%. Why it matters: Analyzing hiring algorithms on a per-position basis is critical when assessing vendor-mediated systems, as aggregate metrics can mask localized discrimination.

docs.title: Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software
docs.text: This case study details how a physicist supervised an AI coding agent (Claude Code, Sonnet, and Opus) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module (CLAX-PT \(\S\)), demonstrating that the supervision protocol, not model capability, was key to achieving trustworthy scientific software. The study identified that the supervision protocol was the primary factor in the agent's output trustworthiness. The agent achieved \(\pm 1\%\) accuracy against the established CLASS-PT benchmark. Why it matters: For scientific AI development, the supervision protocol is as critical as the model's raw capability.

docs.title: Trends & Observations
docs.text:
• There is a trend toward enforcing analytical constraints within neural models, as seen in "Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials" and the explicit geometric constraints in "TriSplat."
  • Control and skill transfer across heterogeneous systems are being addressed by abstracting away physical differences, exemplified by the latent space unification in "Latent Action Diffusion."
  • The role of external, structured optimization is gaining traction; "SkillOpt" demonstrates optimizing procedural knowledge outside the core model weights, while "Physics Is All You Need?" shows external human protocols guiding AI output.
  • Methodological rigor in evaluation is a recurring theme, whether it is using counterfactuals in "Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring" or testing at diverse parameter points in "Physics Is All You Need?".
  • The shift from post-hoc processing to native representation is evident, with "TriSplat" embedding geometry directly into its forward pass structure.

docs.title: This Week in Robotics — 2026, Week 22
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-06-07

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This week's papers touch on several distinct areas of modern AI, from unifying multimodal representations to enhancing robotic control and improving perception pipelines. A recurring theme is the efficacy of targeted, low-overhead modifications: Representation Forcing (RF) in "Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models" allows pixel-space models to match VAE-based counterparts without external components. Similarly, "Exploring Easy Boosts for Lidar Semantic Scene Completion" shows that simple input augmentations, like adding visibility priors, significantly elevate SSC performance. In the realm of continual learning, "TailLoR" introduces a spectral approach to PEFT, steering updates away from dominant components to preserve prior knowledge. Furthermore, "TempoVLA" addresses the need for temporal control in embodied AI, while "Thinking in Blender" demonstrates how structured decomposition enables VLMs to tackle complex, multi-stage inverse graphics tasks.

docs.title: Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models
docs.text:
Representation Forcing (RF) is a technique that enables bottleneck-free unified multimodal models by forcing the decoder to autoregressively predict visual representations as intermediate tokens, guiding pixel diffusion within the same backbone. RF closes the quality gap of pixel-space image generation in UMMs by eliminating the need for a pretrained VAE. The pixel-space model utilizing RF achieved a GenEval Overall score of 0.84, matching the BAGEL baseline (0.82), and reached 0.88 with an LLM rewriter.

Why it matters: RF allows for the creation of end-to-end UMMs by internalizing the latent space, removing reliance on external VAEs.

docs.title: Thinking in Blender: Staged Executable Inverse Graphics with Vision-Language Models
docs.text:
SEIG is a Staged Executable Inverse Graphics framework that reconstructs editable 3D scenes from a single image by progressively refining scene factors—geometry, materials, composition, and lighting—directly within executable Blender code space using a pretrained Vision-Language Model (VLM). SEIG decomposes inverse graphics into sequential stages mirroring professional 3D workflows, such as geometry, material, composition, and lighting. It incorporates a generator-verifier loop within each stage, requiring the verifier to return an explicit approval checklist for convergence.

Why it matters: Task decomposition is crucial for enabling general-purpose VLMs to perform complex inverse graphics tasks effectively.

docs.title: Exploring Easy Boosts for Lidar Semantic Scene Completion
docs.text:
This paper introduces a plug-and-play recipe that significantly boosts Lidar Semantic Scene Completion (SSC) performance by augmenting input point clouds with semantic pseudo-labels and visibility information derived from off-the-shelf techniques. Combining semantic pseudo-labels and visibility information resulted in an mIoU of 33.5, surpassing the SemCity-AE baseline (25.7 mIoU with \(\text{+25.7 mIoU}\)). The combination of these two priors was shown to be complementary, yielding further performance gains.

Why it matters: Simple input augmentations (semantic pseudo-labels and visibility information) can make older SSC models competitive with state-of-the-art systems.

docs.title: TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning
docs.text:
TailLoR is a geometrically aware low-rank adaptation method that uses a soft spectral penalty to protect the dominant singular components of pre-trained weights, thereby routing new task adaptations into the underutilized spectral 'tail' to mitigate interference in continual learning. TailLoR achieves an Overall Accuracy (OA) of 78.87 on the Standard CL Benchmark (Order 1), outperforming the ELLA baseline (78.09). The method preserves the privacy of task-specific parameters as it requires no access to adapters from prior tasks.

Why it matters: When applying PEFT in continual learning, structurally guiding updates based on the singular value decomposition (SVD) of the base weights is more effective than uniform or simple subspace constraints.

docs.title: TempoVLA: Learning Speed-Controllable Vision-Language-Action Policies
docs.text:
TempoVLA introduces a framework to give existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models explicit, bidirectional control over execution speed by combining Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) on the data side and speed conditioning on the model side. The combination of VSTA and speed conditioning consistently lifts the default 1x success rate in simulation and the real world, achieving a Replay success rate (SR) of 97.6% at 1x. The textual prefix method was identified as the simplest and most flexible deployment choice.

Why it matters: Speed control can be added to existing VLAs with minimal engineering effort, as demonstrated by the comparable performance of the three speed injection schemes.

docs.title: Trends & Observations
docs.text:
• There is a clear trend toward augmenting existing systems rather than building entirely new ones, seen in the "Easy Boosts" for Lidar and the speed injection in "TempoVLA."
  • Spectral analysis (SVD) is emerging as a powerful tool for structural modification in PEFT, as demonstrated by "TailLoR" for continual learning.
  • Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) are moving away from rigid latent space assumptions, with "Representation Forcing" enabling end-to-end pixel-space integration.
  • Complex, real-world tasks (like 3D reconstruction) are being addressed by imposing structured, staged workflows onto general-purpose VLMs, as shown in "Thinking in Blender."
  • The integration of temporal control (speed) is becoming a recognized, low-cost extension for embodied AI policies.

docs.title: This Week in Robotics — 2026, Week 23
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-06-14

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This week's research shows a clear trend toward creating more robust, generalist systems by synthesizing specialized knowledge or by intelligently managing information flow. In robotics, HANDOFF addresses the complexity of whole-body control by distilling knowledge from specialized controllers into a compact 10-D command space. Similarly, in generative modeling, Mirage tackles the memory bottleneck in video world models by moving scene representation into the latent space, achieving significant speedups. Multimodal learning is being analyzed theoretically in "When to Align, When to Predict," which provides a framework for deciding between alignment and prediction strategies based on noise characteristics. Furthermore, practical application focuses on making large models more efficient and capable; Reroute offers a training-free way to recover pruned visual tokens in VLMs, while InterleaveThinker uses a multi-agent setup to retrofit image generators for complex sequential tasks.

docs.title: HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers
docs.text:
HANDOFF is a task-space whole-body humanoid controller distilled from three complementary specialists—motion tracking, locomotion, and fall-recovery—to accept a compact, explicit 10-D planner-facing command. The method uses multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme to create a single controller. It demonstrated performance improvements over FALCON [17], achieving \(|\Delta v_x| = 0.06\) and \(|\Delta v_y| = 0.18\).

Why it matters: A compact, explicit 10-D command space allows high-level planners (like VLMs) to interface directly with complex whole-body controllers without requiring dense kinematic streams.

docs.title: UniSHARP: Universal Sharp Monocular View Synthesis
docs.text:
UniSHARP extends SHARP for universal monocular novel view synthesis by aligning images in a unified omnidirectional latent space, enabling rendering across perspective, wide-FoV, fisheye, and panoramic camera systems. The framework reformulates SHARP-style Gaussian prediction in ray-distance space and fuses 2D semantic encodings with 3D spatial features. This resulted in a PSNR of 21.556 and an SSIM of 0.674 compared to SHARP [12].

Why it matters: For universal monocular synthesis, shifting representation from image-plane coordinates to a ray-distance space is crucial for handling heterogeneous camera models.

docs.title: Latent Spatial Memory for Video World Models
docs.text:
Mirage introduces latent spatial memory, a persistent 3D cache storing scene information directly in the diffusion latent space, to enable geometrically consistent video world models while avoiding the computational and representational bottlenecks of traditional RGB point-cloud memories. Mirage utilizes depth-guided back-projection and occlusion-aware readout at latent resolution. This approach yielded an end-to-end video generation speedup of \(10.57\times\) faster than RGB point-cloud readout.

Why it matters: For 3D-consistent video generation, moving memory storage from pixel space (RGB) to latent space significantly reduces computational overhead.

docs.title: When to Align, When to Predict: A Phase Diagram for Multimodal Learning
docs.text:
This paper develops a unified linear framework and phase diagram to systematically determine when Cross-modal Alignment (CA) or Cross-modal Prediction (CP) succeeds or fails in multimodal representation learning under a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation. The authors derived separation ratios \(\Delta_{CA}\) and \(\Delta_{CP}\) under the specified model. The analysis showed a subspace distance of \(\nu \approx 0.75\) for CA robustness versus \(\nu \approx 0.15\) for CP failure.

Why it matters: CA is preferable when nuisance correlation is moderate, modality quality is uncertain, and modality-specific noise is large.

docs.title: Reroute, Don't Remove: Recoverable Visual Token Routing for Vision-Language Models
docs.text:
Reroute introduces a training-free plug-in that replaces irreversible visual token removal in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with recoverable routing, allowing deferred tokens to re-enter the candidate pool at subsequent decoder stages. The plug-in preserves the FLOPs and KV-cache budget of augmented pruning methods. On LLaVA-1.5-7B, it achieved an IoU of \(0.80\) compared to \(0.38\) for PDrop.

Why it matters: If your VLM token reduction method relies on permanent deletion, consider Reroute to recover lost visual evidence.

docs.title: InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation
docs.text:
InterleaveThinker is a novel multi-agent pipeline that endows any existing image generator with strong interleaved generation capabilities by employing a Planner agent to sequence instructions and a Critic agent to enforce step-wise correction. The framework uses a dual-reward strategy (accuracy and step-wise) to achieve trajectory-level alignment via GRPO. This resulted in a WISE score of \(0.73\) compared to a baseline of \(0.47\).

Why it matters: Multi-agent frameworks can effectively retrofit existing, frozen image generators to handle complex sequential tasks like visual narratives.

docs.title: Learning to Reason by Analogy via Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning
docs.text:
RA-RFT is a post-training framework that enhances language model reasoning by using gold-relevance distillation to train a retriever that selects reasoning-analogous examples, which are then used to fine-tune the policy model via reinforcement learning. The retriever is trained to rank contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic overlap. This led to an AIME 2025 average@32 accuracy of \(7.1\) points when compared to \(2.8\) points using GRPO.

Why it matters: For complex reasoning tasks, grounding retrieval in 'reasoning utility' (analogical fit) is more effective than relying on surface-level semantic similarity.

docs.title: Trends & Observations
docs.text:
• There is a strong architectural trend toward distillation and synthesis, exemplified by HANDOFF creating a generalist controller from specialized components.
  • Memory management in generative models is shifting from high-dimensional pixel space to lower-dimensional latent space (Mirage), which offers substantial computational savings.
  • Theoretical work is providing prescriptive guidance for multimodal systems, suggesting that the choice between alignment and prediction depends on the underlying noise structure ("When to Align, When to Predict").
  • Efficiency improvements in large models are moving beyond simple pruning to methods that allow for information recovery (Reroute).
  • Complex agentic behavior is being achieved by decoupling planning from execution/evaluation using multi-agent architectures (InterleaveThinker).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Hand-written notes and write-ups.

docs.title: Actuator DesignActuator Design for Robots
docs.text:
Actuator Design for Robots — Study Notes Robotics Engineering · Study Notes

From motor physics to transmission selection, thermal limits, and actuation paradigms — everything needed to specify and design a robot joint actuator, with a worked sizing example for a physical-interaction arm joint.

1What an actuator must do 2Writing the requirements 3Actuation technologies 4Electric motor fundamentals 5Transmissions & reflected inertia 6Actuation paradigms 7Sensing & drive electronics 8Thermal design 9The design procedure 10Worked example: arm joint 11Safety for physical HRI 12Further reading 1What an actuator must do

An actuator is the complete chain that converts stored energy into controlled mechanical work at a joint. In a modern robot it is never just "a motor": it is the power electronics, the electromagnetic machine, the transmission, the bearings and structure, and the sensors that close the loop. Designing an actuator means co-designing all five so that the joint delivers the right torque, at the right speed, with the right dynamic behavior (bandwidth, impedance, backdrivability), inside mass, volume, thermal, and cost budgets.

Fig. 1.1The actuator chain Energy source battery / supply Drive electronics inverter + FOC Motor elec → mech Transmission ratio N : 1 Load / joint link + environment V, I PWM τm, ωm τj, ωj Controller (current / velocity / position / torque loops) encoder, I θ out τ sense (opt.) commands Every block transforms power and adds losses, inertia, and dynamics. The design problem is allocating the requirements across the chain — most importantly choosing the split between motor torque and transmission ratio. Central tension Electric motors are naturally fast and weak; robot joints need slow and strong. Almost every design decision is about how to bridge that gap — and what you sacrifice (transparency, efficiency, stiffness, mass, cost) in doing so. 2Writing the requirements

Actuator design starts from the load side, never from a motor catalog. For each joint, derive a torque–speed operating envelope from the robot's kinematics, payload, and motion profile:

  • Peak torque τpk — worst-case static gravity torque plus dynamic torque (J·α for accelerations, impacts, disturbance rejection). Typically needed for seconds.
  • Continuous (RMS) torque τrms — computed over the duty cycle; this is what sets the thermal design, and it is the spec beginners miss.
  • Maximum joint speed ωmax and the speed at which peak torque must still be available (corner power point).
  • Bandwidth — how fast the joint must track torque or position commands (manipulation ~5–20 Hz closed-loop; legged locomotion impact response is much higher).
  • Backdrivability / transparency — can the environment move the joint? Critical for contact-rich tasks, safety around humans, and proprioceptive force sensing.
  • Accuracy & stiffness — backlash, torque ripple, positioning repeatability.
  • Envelope budgets — mass (it multiplies down a serial arm: distal actuator mass costs proximal torque), diameter/length, voltage available, thermal environment, noise, ingress protection, cost.

The RMS torque over a cycle of duration T is:

τrms = √( (1/T) ∫0T τ(t)² dt )EQ 2.1 — thermal-equivalent torque

A useful discipline: plot every operating point of the motion profile on the torque–speed plane. The actuator is feasible only if the continuous envelope covers the RMS point and the peak envelope covers every instantaneous point.

3Survey of actuation technologies

Electromagnetic actuators dominate robotics, but knowing the alternatives clarifies why — and when to deviate.

Table 3.1 — Actuation technologies compared TechnologyPower densityBandwidthControl fidelityEfficiencyTypical use BLDC / PMSM electricHigh (w/ gearing)HighExcellent (FOC)70–95%Nearly all modern robots Brushed DCModerateHighGood, simple drive~60–80%Hobby, low-cost, legacy StepperLowModerateOpen-loop positionPoor at speed3D printers, light automation HydraulicVery highHighHard (valve nonlin.)Low system-levelHeavy legged robots, excavators PneumaticModerateLowCompressible, springyLowGrippers, soft robots, PAMs PiezoelectricLow (tiny stroke)Very highSub-µm precision—Micro-positioning, optics SMA / artificial muscleHigh force/massVery lowHysteretic<5%Research, niche soft robotics

Hydraulics win on raw force density and impact robustness (early Atlas, excavator-scale machines) but lose on efficiency, cleanliness, and controllability; the field's broad move to electric actuation (e.g., electric Atlas, all commercial quadrupeds and cobots) reflects how good BLDC + power electronics + control have become. The rest of these notes assume electromagnetic actuation, the default for >95% of robot joints.

4Electric motor fundamentals 4.1The two constants that define a motor

For a permanent-magnet machine (brushed DC, or a BLDC/PMSM under field-oriented control, which behaves like an ideal DC machine in the d–q frame):

τm = Kt · I      V = I·R + L·dI/dt + Ke · ωmEQ 4.1 — torque & voltage equations

Kt (N·m/A) maps current to torque; Ke (V·s/rad) is the back-EMF constant. In consistent SI units, Kt = Ke — one physical constant seen from two sides. Consequences:

  • Torque is bought with current → copper (I²R) heating sets the continuous torque limit.
  • Speed is bought with voltage → bus voltage and Ke set the no-load speed ωnl ≈ V/Ke.
4.2The torque–speed curve

At fixed voltage, steady-state torque falls linearly with speed:

τ(ω) = (Kt/R)·(V − Keω) = τstall·(1 − ω/ωnl)EQ 4.2 — torque–speed line Fig. 4.1Torque–speed plane of a PM motor τ_stall (peak, thermally time-limited) τ_cont (steady-state thermal limit) P = τ·ω = const. ω_nl CONTINUOUS INTERMITTENT (seconds) infeasible torque τ speed ω RMS operating point must sit here Two envelopes matter: the peak line (limited by drive current, demagnetization, supply) usable for seconds, and the continuous line (limited by steady-state heat rejection). Datasheet "rated" values assume a specific heatsink — always check the test conditions. 4.3Copper loss and the motor constant Km

The dominant loss at robot-joint speeds is resistive heating. Substituting I = τ/Kt:

Pcu = I²R = τ² · (R/Kt²) = (τ / Km)²,   where  Km ≜ Kt/√REQ 4.3 — copper loss & motor constant

Km (N·m/√W) is the single best figure of merit for comparing motors: torque per square root of heat. It is independent of winding (rewinding a motor changes Kt and R together, leaving Km fixed) — winding choice only trades voltage against current for the same physics. Compare motors by Km/mass or Km/volume, not by Kt.

4.4Where torque comes from: geometry

Torque scales with air-gap area times gap radius — roughly τ ∝ rgap² · L for fixed magnetic and current loading. This is why high-torque robot actuators use large-diameter, short ("pancake") outrunner motors with high pole counts: a big gap radius buys torque directly, reducing the gear ratio needed downstream. Frameless torque-motor kits (rotor + stator supplied without housing) let you integrate the magnetics directly into the joint structure.

Motor selection heuristics ① Shortlist by Km/kg and diameter fit. ② Check τcont with your heatsinking, not the datasheet's. ③ Check ωnl = Vbus/Ke covers required speed after gearing, with ~20–30% voltage headroom for current-loop authority. ④ Check peak current is within drive and demagnetization limits. 5Transmissions & reflected inertia 5.1What a gear ratio buys — and what it costs

A transmission of ratio N multiplies torque and divides speed:

τout = N · η · τm     ωout = ωm / NEQ 5.1 — ideal transmission with efficiency η

But seen from the output, the rotor's inertia and friction are magnified by N²:

Jreflected = N² · Jrotor     breflected = N² · bmEQ 5.2 — reflected inertia & damping Fig. 5.1Reflection through a gearbox rotor: J_rotor, τ_m, ω_m gearbox N : 1, η load J_load seen at output: τ = N·η·τ_m J = N²·J_rotor b = N²·b_m Torque scales with N — but inertia and friction scale with N². At N = 100, a 10⁻⁴ kg·m² rotor looks like 1 kg·m². The N² law is the deep reason high gear ratios destroy backdrivability and contact transparency: the environment must accelerate N² times the rotor inertia and fight N² times its friction to move the joint.

Two more classical results follow from the N² law:

  • Inertia matching: for maximum load acceleration with a given motor, choose N = √(Jload/Jrotor) — the ratio that makes reflected rotor inertia equal load inertia. Real designs often sit below this (favoring transparency) or above it (favoring static torque).
  • Torque sensing fidelity: with low N, motor current is an honest estimate of joint torque (proprioception); with high N, friction and inertia swamp it, forcing a separate torque sensor or spring.
5.2Transmission families Table 5.1 — Common robot transmissions TypeRatio / stageBacklashEfficiencyBackdrivableNotes Spur / belt1–5Low–mod95–98%YesSimple, cheap; belts add compliance, zero backlash Planetary3–10Moderate~97%/stageYes (low N)Coaxial, torque-dense; the QDD workhorse Harmonic (strain-wave)50–160≈ Zero60–85%PoorlyCompact, precise; flexspline compliance & ratcheting limits; standard in cobots Cycloidal30–120Very low85–93%PoorlyHigh shock capacity & stiffness; some torque ripple; industrial robot wrists/bases Ball screwlinearLow~90%SometimesRotary→linear; prosthetics, legs, lifts Cable / capstan5–20Zero~95%ExcellentSmooth, transparent, remote-izes motor mass; routing complexity, cable maintenance Fig. 5.2Three gearbox topologies (schematic) sun planet Planetary N = 1 + ring/sun · 3–10 per stage wave gen. flexspline Strain-wave (harmonic) N = 50–160, zero backlash, compliant cycloid disc Cycloidal N = 30–120, very stiff, shock-tolerant Planetary stages give modest ratios with good efficiency and backdrivability; strain-wave and cycloidal drives give huge single-stage ratios at the cost of transparency. Eccentric motion in the cycloidal disc engages many lobes simultaneously — hence its shock capacity. 6Actuation paradigms

Combining the motor and transmission choices yields four recognizable design philosophies. The axis that separates them is how the actuator handles contact with the world.

Fig. 6.1The paradigm map contact transparency / backdrivability transmission ratio N → Direct drive N = 1 · heavy, hot Quasi-direct drive (QDD) N ≈ 5–10 · proprioceptive Series elastic (SEA) high N + spring · bandwidth-limited High-ratio geared N = 50–160 (+ joint torque sensor in cobots) torque density grows → ↑ transparency recovered via spring or torque sensor + control Raising N raises torque density but buries the load behind N² rotor dynamics. Each paradigm is a different answer to recovering force control: big magnetics (DD/QDD), a deliberate spring (SEA), or sensors + software (geared cobots). 6.1Direct drive (N = 1)

Motor torque = joint torque. Perfect transparency, zero backlash, current ≈ torque exactly. But torque per mass is poor — to hold meaningful loads the motors become large, heavy, and hot (continuous torque costs (τ/Km)² in heat). Used where transparency matters more than gravity loads: haptic devices, some SCARA arms, gimbals.

6.2Quasi-direct drive / proprioceptive actuation (N ≈ 5–10)

The MIT Mini Cheetah pattern, now ubiquitous in legged robots and low-cost arms: a large-gap-radius outrunner (exploiting τ ∝ rgap²) plus a single planetary stage. Reflected inertia stays small (N² = 25–100, not 10,000), so the joint is backdrivable, survives impacts by letting the rotor accelerate, and senses contact through motor current alone — "proprioceptive" force control with no extra sensor. Costs: lower torque density than harmonic designs, more heat for the same continuous torque, large diameter joints.

Fig. 6.2QDD actuator cross-section (schematic) axis of rotation housing (stator carrier, heat path) rotor: magnet ring (outrunner, large r_gap) stator: laminations + copper windings r_gap (τ ∝ r²) planetary N ≈ 6 : 1 output flange output bearing rotor encoder (for FOC) Pancake geometry: the magnetics do most of the torque work so the gearbox can stay small. One rotor-side encoder serves commutation and (divided by N) joint position; premium designs add an output-side absolute encoder. 6.3High-ratio geared (harmonic / cycloidal, N = 50–160)

The industrial-arm and cobot standard: a compact frameless motor plus a strain-wave or cycloidal stage gives outstanding torque density and stiffness with near-zero backlash. Transparency is gone — so cobots (KUKA iiwa, Franka) add a joint torque sensor after the gearbox and close a torque loop to fake backdrivability in software. Limits: torque control bandwidth is bounded by structural/gear dynamics, and unsensed collisions are felt through N² inertia before control reacts.

6.4Series elastic actuation (SEA)

Insert a known spring (stiffness ks) between gearbox and load and measure its deflection: τ = ks·Δθ. The spring turns torque control into position control of deflection, filters impacts, and stores energy (great for walking). The cost is bandwidth — the motor must move the spring before torque changes, so force bandwidth falls with softer springs. Classic in humanoids (early Valkyrie, Baxter) and prosthetics/exoskeletons.

Fig. 6.3Series elastic actuator concept motor gearbox N:1 spring k_s · measure Δθ load τ = k_s · Δθ torque from deflection The spring converts a stiff, opaque geared drive into a clean torque source — at the price of force bandwidth. Variants: parallel-elastic (spring assists gravity load), variable stiffness actuators (VSA), and clutched designs. Spring stiffness selection is a bandwidth-vs-robustness trade. Table 6.1 — Choosing a paradigm If the priority is…ChooseExamples Positioning accuracy & stiffness, structured environmentHigh-ratio geared (harmonic/cycloidal)Industrial arms Safe human contact with precise motionGeared + joint torque sensingCobots (iiwa, Franka) Impacts, dynamic locomotion, proprioceptive contactQDDMini Cheetah, most quadrupeds & new humanoid legs Compliant interaction, energy storage, shock toleranceSEAExoskeletons, prosthetics, some humanoids Maximum transparency, low loadsDirect drive / capstanHaptics, teleop masters 7Sensing & drive electronics 7.1Field-oriented control (FOC)

A BLDC/PMSM is driven by a three-phase inverter. FOC transforms phase currents into the rotor frame (Clarke/Park transforms) and regulates the torque-producing current iq with a PI loop running at 10–40 kHz. Result: smooth torque proportional to iq, just like a DC machine. The cascade above it is conventional: current (kHz) → velocity (hundreds of Hz) → position (tens–hundreds of Hz), each loop ~5–10× slower than the one inside it. Modern integrated driver boards (e.g. moteus, ODrive-class, or vendor servo drives) make this a solved subsystem — your job is mostly parameter identification and tuning.

7.2Encoders
  • Rotor-side encoder (mandatory): commutation + velocity. Magnetic off-axis or on-axis (e.g. 14–17 bit) is standard; optical for precision.
  • Output-side absolute encoder (recommended for arms): measures the joint after backlash/compliance, gives true link angle and absolute startup position. Dual-encoder joints can also estimate transmission deflection → crude torque sensing.
  • Joint torque sensor (cobots): strain-gauge structure after the gearbox; adds cost and structural compliance but enables high-fidelity torque control through opaque transmissions.
7.3Electrical system notes
  • Bus voltage sets the speed ceiling (ωnl = V/Ke); 24/48 V is typical for arms and small robots. Higher voltage → lower current → thinner cables, less I²R in wiring.
  • Regeneration: a decelerating joint pumps energy back into the bus — budget a brake resistor or battery that can absorb it, or the bus voltage will spike.
  • Current limits live in three places: motor demagnetization, drive FETs, and supply. The firmware limit must respect all three.
8Thermal design

Continuous torque is a thermal spec. The winding temperature rise above ambient is, in the simplest lumped model:

ΔT = Ploss · Rth = (τrms/Km)² · (Rth,wh + Rth,ha)EQ 8.1 — lumped thermal model

where Rth,wh is winding→housing and Rth,ha housing→ambient thermal resistance (K/W). Design levers, in order of leverage:

  1. Lower the heat: pick a higher-Km motor or raise N (heat falls with the square of required motor torque).
  2. Shrink Rth,ha: bolt the stator to structure with real conducted area; the robot's frame is your heatsink. Potting windings helps Rth,wh.
  3. Exploit transients: thermal time constants are tens of seconds (winding) to minutes (housing) — peak torque bursts are fine if the RMS is safe. Implement an I²t thermal observer in firmware rather than a hard current cap.
  4. Remember R rises with temperature (copper +0.4%/K): a hot motor produces less torque per volt and more heat per amp — thermal runaway is real near the limit.
9The design procedure Fig. 9.1Actuator design workflow 1 · Load analysis τ–ω envelope, τ_pk, τ_rms, duty cycle 2 · Paradigm & transmission family transparency vs torque density vs precision 3 · Choose ratio N speed margin, reflected inertia, sensing fidelity 4 · Select motor τ_cont, τ_pk, K_m/kg, diameter, V_bus speed check 5 · Thermal & electrical check ΔT at τ_rms · drive current · regen budget 6 · Integrate & verify bearings, encoders, dyno test, thermal soak fails? iterate N ↔ motor measured data refines requirements Steps 3–4–5 form the inner loop: ratio and motor are chosen jointly, and the thermal check usually forces one or two iterations. Always finish on a dynamometer, not a datasheet.

Feasibility conditions to satisfy simultaneously (with efficiency η and margin factors):

N·η·τm,pk ≥ τpk   ·   N·η·τm,cont ≥ τrms   ·   ωnl/N ≥ ~1.3·ωmaxEQ 9.1 — feasibility set 10Worked example: a physical-interaction arm joint

Size the elbow/shoulder-class joint of an assistive arm that must support and reposition a limb-scale load while remaining backdrivable enough for safe human contact.

SIZING WALKTHROUGH — assistive arm joint 01Requirements. Gravity load: 10 kg effective mass at 0.5 m lever → τg ≈ 10·9.81·0.5 ≈ 49 N·m. Add dynamics and margin: τpk = 60 N·m. Duty-cycle RMS over a transfer-assist motion (hold + move + idle): τrms = 25 N·m. Max joint speed ωmax = 2 rad/s (≈115°/s — deliberately slow around people). Backdrivability required → cap the ratio at N ≲ 30; safety argues against harmonic-only designs without torque sensing. 02Candidate motor. Frameless outrunner, Kt = 0.11 N·m/A, R = 0.15 Ω (→ Km = 0.28 N·m/√W), τcont = 1.2 N·m (heatsunk), τpk = 3.5 N·m, Jrotor = 1.2×10⁻⁴ kg·m². 03Gear ratio. With two-stage planetary η ≈ 0.85: peak needs N ≥ 60/(0.85·3.5) ≈ 20.2; continuous needs N ≥ 25/(0.85·1.2) ≈ 24.5. Choose N = 25. 04Speed check. Motor speed at ωmax: 25 × 2 = 50 rad/s ≈ 478 rpm — trivially low; even a 24 V bus gives ωnl = 24/0.11 ≈ 218 rad/s, a 4× margin. ✔ (We could trade: a lower-Kt winding would lower current; here current is the binding constraint instead.) 05Currents. Peak motor torque 60/(25·0.85) = 2.82 N·m → Ipk = 2.82/0.11 ≈ 26 A (drive must supply this). Continuous 25/(25·0.85) = 1.18 N·m → Icont ≈ 10.7 A. 06Thermal check. Pcu = (1.18/0.28)² ≈ 17.8 W. With stator bolted to the joint casting, Rth ≈ 2 K/W → ΔT ≈ 36 K: winding ~60–65 °C in a 25 °C room. ✔ but note τcont usage is at 98% of rating — no margin. Action: either accept with an I²t observer and conservative duty assumptions, step up one motor size, or add a parallel gravity-compensation spring to cut τrms (often the single best trick for joints that hold loads against gravity). 07Reflected inertia & transparency. Jrefl = 25² × 1.2×10⁻⁴ = 0.075 kg·m² — comparable to the link's own inertia: moderately backdrivable, adequate current-based collision detection, much safer than an N = 100 harmonic joint (which would reflect 1.2 kg·m²). For higher contact fidelity, add an output torque sensor or drop to N ≈ 10 with a larger motor. 08Spec the rest. Crossed-roller output bearing (moment loads), 14-bit rotor encoder + absolute output encoder, 48 V bus to halve currents if cabling is long, FOC drive with ≥30 A peak, brake resistor sized for the worst lowering-a-load regen event, and — for an assistive device — a power-off brake or self-locking backup path so the joint fails safe under gravity. 11Safety considerations for physical HRI

When the actuator works on or around people, design choices become safety arguments:

  • Limit energy, not just force. ISO/TS 15066-style thinking bounds transferred energy and pressure in contact; low reflected inertia (low N) directly reduces impact severity before any controller reacts.
  • Layered torque limits: firmware current clamp → drive hardware limit → mechanical fuse/slip clutch. Software limits alone are not a safety case.
  • Fail-safe under gravity: power-off brakes, self-locking stages, or counterbalancing so loss of power never drops the load.
  • Collision detection: momentum-observer or current-residual methods work well on transparent (QDD) joints; geared joints need torque sensors for comparable sensitivity.
  • Redundant sensing (dual encoders, cross-checked) for any joint whose failure can injure.
12Further reading
  • Hunter, Hollerbach & Ballantyne, "A comparative analysis of actuator technologies for robotics" — the classic technology survey framing.
  • Seok, Wang, Otten & Kim, "Actuator design for high force proprioceptive control in fast legged locomotion" (IROS 2012) — the gap-radius / QDD argument.
  • Wensing et al., "Proprioceptive actuator design in the MIT Cheetah" (T-RO 2017) — impact mitigation, reflected inertia, thermal analysis in depth.
  • Katz, "A low cost modular actuator for dynamic robots" (MIT thesis, 2018) — a complete, readable QDD design + build, including drive electronics.
  • Pratt & Williamson, "Series elastic actuators" (IROS 1995) — the SEA origin paper.
  • Albu-Schäffer et al., DLR lightweight robot papers — torque-sensed harmonic-drive joints and impedance control (the cobot lineage).
  • Hurst & collaborators on compliant legged actuation; Haddadin on collision detection and safe pHRI.
Study notes · Actuator Design for Robots · Equations in SI units throughout (τ in N·m, ω in rad/s, Kt = Ke numerically in SI). Datasheet values vary by test condition — verify continuous ratings against your own thermal path.

docs.title: Research Digest
docs.text: AI-generated summaries of top robotics and ML papers, powered by Gemma 4 running locally.

docs.title: MemoryWAM: Efficient World Action Modeling with Persistent Memory
docs.text:
2026-06-21

MemoryWAM introduces a hybrid memory mechanism combining sliding-window context, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens to enable efficient persistent memory in World Action Models, reducing complexity from O(N) to O(N/d).

docs.title: JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising
docs.text:
2026-06-20

JanusMesh presents a fast and training-free framework that generates text-driven 3D visual illusions by decoupling the process into a cross-space dual-branch denoising stage for geometry and a view-conditioned texture synthesis stage for appearance.

docs.title: Unified Multimodal Autoregressive Modeling with Shared Context-Visual Tokenizer is Key to Unification
docs.text:
2026-06-18

UniAR is a unified autoregressive framework that uses a single discrete visual tokenizer, leveraging multi-level feature fusion and lookup-free bitwise quantization, to integrate visual understanding and generation in a shared context.

docs.title: T-Rex: Tactile-Reactive Dexterous Manipulation
docs.text:
2026-06-17

T-Rex is a tactile-reactive dexterous manipulation framework utilizing a variable-rate Mixture-of-Transformer-Experts (MoT) architecture, trained on a 100-hour tactile-rich dataset, to achieve high success rates in contact-rich tasks.

docs.title: SIMMER: Benchmarking Latent Failures in LLM Executable Planning with a World Model
docs.text:
2026-06-16

SIMMER is a new benchmark that evaluates latent failures in LLM executable planning by grounding execution against a symbolic world model derived from real-world cooking scripts.

docs.title: Mana: Dexterous Manipulation of Articulated Tools
docs.text:
2026-06-15

MANA (Manipulation Animator) is a general sim-to-real framework that reinterprets dexterous articulated tool manipulation as a coarse-to-fine animation problem to achieve zero-shot transfer.

docs.title: Learning to Reason by Analogy via Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning
docs.text:
2026-06-14

RA-RFT is a post-training framework that enhances language model reasoning by using gold-relevance distillation to train a retriever that selects reasoning-analogous examples, which are then used to fine-tune the policy model via reinforcement learning.

docs.title: InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation
docs.text:
2026-06-13

InterleaveThinker is a novel multi-agent pipeline that endows any existing image generator with strong interleaved generation capabilities by employing a Planner agent to sequence instructions and a Critic agent to enforce step-wise correction.

docs.title: Reroute, Don't Remove: Recoverable Visual Token Routing for Vision-Language Models
docs.text:
2026-06-12

Reroute introduces a training-free plug-in that replaces irreversible visual token removal in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with recoverable routing, allowing deferred tokens to re-enter the candidate pool at subsequent decoder stages.

docs.title: When to Align, When to Predict: A Phase Diagram for Multimodal Learning
docs.text:
2026-06-11

This paper develops a unified linear framework and phase diagram to systematically determine when Cross-modal Alignment (CA) or Cross-modal Prediction (CP) succeeds or fails in multimodal representation learning under a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation.

docs.title: Latent Spatial Memory for Video World Models
docs.text:
2026-06-10

Mirage introduces latent spatial memory, a persistent 3D cache storing scene information directly in the diffusion latent space, to enable geometrically consistent video world models while avoiding the computational and representational bottlenecks of traditional RGB point-cloud memories.

docs.title: UniSHARP: Universal Sharp Monocular View Synthesis
docs.text:
2026-06-09

UniSHARP extends SHARP for universal monocular novel view synthesis by aligning images in a unified omnidirectional latent space, enabling rendering across perspective, wide-FoV, fisheye, and panoramic camera systems.

docs.title: HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers
docs.text:
2026-06-08

HANDOFF is a task-space whole-body humanoid controller distilled from three complementary specialists—motion tracking, locomotion, and fall-recovery—to accept a compact, explicit 10-D planner-facing command.

docs.title: TempoVLA: Learning Speed-Controllable Vision-Language-Action Policies
docs.text:
2026-06-07

TempoVLA introduces a framework to give existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models explicit, bidirectional control over execution speed by combining Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) on the data side and speed conditioning on the model side.

docs.title: TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning
docs.text:
2026-06-06

TailLoR is a geometrically aware low-rank adaptation method that uses a soft spectral penalty to protect the dominant singular components of pre-trained weights, thereby routing new task adaptations into the underutilized spectral 'tail' to mitigate interference in continual learning.

docs.title: Exploring Easy Boosts for Lidar Semantic Scene Completion
docs.text:
2026-06-04

This paper introduces a plug-and-play recipe that significantly boosts Lidar Semantic Scene Completion (SSC) performance by augmenting input point clouds with semantic pseudo-labels and visibility information derived from off-the-shelf techniques.

docs.title: Thinking in Blender: Staged Executable Inverse Graphics with Vision-Language Models
docs.text:
2026-06-03

SEIG is a Staged Executable Inverse Graphics framework that reconstructs editable 3D scenes from a single image by progressively refining scene factors—geometry, materials, composition, and lighting—directly within executable Blender code space using a pretrained Vision-Language Model (VLM).

docs.title: Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models
docs.text:
2026-06-02

Representation Forcing (RF) is a technique that enables bottleneck-free unified multimodal models by forcing the decoder to autoregressively predict visual representations as intermediate tokens, guiding pixel diffusion within the same backbone.

docs.title: Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software
docs.text:
2026-05-31

This case study details how a physicist supervised an AI coding agent (Claude Code, Sonnet, and Opus) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module (CLAX-PT §), demonstrating that the supervision protocol, not model capability, was key to achieving trustworthy scientific software.

docs.title: Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring
docs.text:
2026-05-28

This study investigates algorithmic monoculture in hiring by analyzing a novel dataset from the vendor pymetrics, finding clear racial disparities and systemic rejection patterns when examining hiring decisions on a per-position basis.

docs.title: TriSplat: Simulation-Ready Feed-Forward 3D Scene Reconstruction
docs.text:
2026-05-27

TriSplat is a feed-forward 3D scene reconstruction network that uses oriented triangle primitives to directly export simulation-ready mesh scenes from sparse, unposed images in a single forward pass.

docs.title: Latent Action Diffusion for Cross-Embodiment Manipulation
docs.text:
2026-05-27

This paper introduces Latent Action Diffusion to unify diverse end-effector action spaces into a semantically aligned latent space, enabling cross-embodiment policy learning for multi-robot control and skill transfer.

docs.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
docs.text:
2026-05-26

SkillOpt introduces a systematic, controllable text-space optimizer that treats agent skills as an external state, allowing for deep-learning-style optimization of procedural knowledge without modifying the frozen target model weights.

docs.title: Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials
docs.text:
2026-05-25

The Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN) is a demand-first neural model that represents log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing elasticities to be derived exactly as derivatives of the learned demand surface.

docs.title: Which Way Did It Move? Diagnosing and Overcoming Directional Motion Blindness in Video-LLMs
docs.text:
2026-05-24

The paper introduces MODIRECT and DeltaDirect to diagnose and overcome directional motion blindness in Video-LLMs, which stems from a direction binding gap where motion signals are present but not reliably linked to verbal answers.

docs.title: Tokenisation via Convex Relaxations
docs.text:
2026-05-23

ConvexTok formulates tokeniser construction as a linear program relaxation of an NP-hard integer program, allowing for the computation of near-optimal tokenisers and providing lower bounds on compression.

docs.title: Explicit Stair Geometry Conditioning for Robust Humanoid Locomotion
docs.text:
2026-05-23

This paper introduces an explicit stair geometry conditioning framework that uses interpretable parameters like step height, step depth, and current yaw angle to condition a PPO-based locomotion policy for robust humanoid stair climbing.

docs.title: Variance Reduction for Expectations with Diffusion Teachers
docs.text:
2026-05-22

CARV is a compute-aware variance-accounting framework that introduces a hierarchical Monte Carlo estimator by amortizing expensive upstream computations over cheap diffusion-noise resamples, sharpened by timestep importance sampling and stratified-inverse-CDF construction.

docs.title: Can These Views Be One Scene? Evaluating Multiview 3D Consistency when 3D Foundation Models Hallucinate
docs.text:
2026-05-20

This paper introduces SysCON3D, a controlled benchmark, and a parametric family of neural metrics to diagnose and improve the robustness of ground-truth-free multiview 3D consistency evaluators against geometric hallucinations.

docs.title: V-JEPA 2.1: Unlocking Dense Features in Video Self-Supervised Learning
docs.text:
2026-05-18

V-JEPA 2.1 enhances self-supervised learning for dense visual understanding in images and videos by applying a Dense Predictive Loss across all tokens and incorporating Deep Self-Supervision.

docs.title: RefDecoder: Enhancing Visual Generation with Conditional Video Decoding
docs.text:
2026-05-18

REFDECODER enhances visual generation by introducing a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder that injects high-fidelity reference image signals directly into the decoding process via reference attention, mitigating detail loss and inconsistency inherent in unconditional VAE decoders.

docs.title: ATLAS: Agentic or Latent Visual Reasoning? One Word is Enough for Both
docs.text:
2026-05-17

ATLAS is a framework that unifies agentic and latent visual reasoning by representing visual operations as discrete functional tokens within a standard autoregressive sequence, stabilized by Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO) during RL training.

docs.title: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
docs.text:
2026-05-16

DreamZero is a World Action Model (WAM) built on a pretrained video diffusion backbone that jointly predicts future video states and actions, enabling zero-shot generalization and efficient cross-embodiment transfer from diverse, non-repetitive data.

docs.title: EntityBench: Towards Entity-Consistent Long-Range Multi-Shot Video Generation
docs.text:
2026-05-16

EntityBench introduces a new benchmark for multi-shot video generation that rigorously evaluates entity consistency across characters, objects, and locations using a three-pillar framework, and proposes EntityMem, a memory-augmented system to enhance this consistency.

docs.title: EVA-Bench: A New End-to-end Framework for Evaluating Voice Agents
docs.text:
2026-05-15

EVA-Bench is an end-to-end evaluation framework that addresses the dual challenges of generating realistic, validated bot-to-bot audio conversations and measuring voice agent quality across comprehensive, voice-specific failure modes using composite metrics EVA-A and EVA-X.

docs.title: Benchmarking Vision, Language, & Action Models on Robotic Learning Tasks
docs.text:
2026-05-14

This work presents a comprehensive evaluation framework and benchmark suite, MultiNet v0.1, to systematically assess the capabilities and limitations of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like GPT-4o, OpenVLA, and JAT across 20 diverse real-world robotic tasks from the Open-X-Embodiment collection.

docs.title: Normalizing Trajectory Models
docs.text:
2026-05-12

Normalizing Trajectory Models (NTM) introduces a framework that models the non-Gaussian reverse conditional \(p(x_s | x_t)\) as a conditional normalizing flow, enabling exact likelihood training for high-quality, few-step image generation.

docs.title: BAMI: Training-Free Bias Mitigation in GUI Grounding
docs.text:
2026-05-10

BAMI introduces a training-free inference framework that mitigates precision and ambiguity biases in GUI grounding by employing coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection manipulations.

docs.title: \(π_0\): A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control
docs.text:
2026-05-10

π0 is a novel flow matching architecture built on a pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM) to create a generalist robot policy capable of performing complex, dexterous tasks via direct prompting or fine-tuning.

docs.title: World Model for Robot Learning: A Comprehensive Survey
docs.text:
2026-05-09

This survey provides a comprehensive, robot-learning-centered review of world models, detailing their coupling with robot policies, their function as simulators, and their application in robotic video generation.

docs.title: ActCam: Zero-Shot Joint Camera and 3D Motion Control for Video Generation
docs.text:
2026-05-09

ActCam is a zero-shot, training-free framework that achieves joint control over character motion and camera trajectory in image-conditioned video generation by constructing camera-aligned pose and depth conditioning signals.

docs.title: Audio-Visual Intelligence in Large Foundation Models
docs.text:
2026-05-07

This survey provides the first comprehensive review of Audio-Visual Intelligence (AVI) within the paradigm of large foundation models, unifying perception, generation, and interaction research under a coherent framework.

docs.title: AlbumFill: Album-Guided Reasoning and Retrieval for Personalized Image Completion
docs.text:
2026-05-06

AlbumFill is a training-free framework that uses vision-language reasoning to automatically retrieve identity-consistent reference images from personal photo albums to guide personalized image completion.

docs.title: Posterior Augmented Flow Matching
docs.text:
2026-05-05

Posterior-Augmented Flow Matching (PAFM) generalizes standard Flow Matching (FM) by replacing single-target supervision with an expectation over an approximate posterior of valid target completions, thereby reducing gradient variance.

docs.title: HERMES++: Toward a Unified Driving World Model for 3D Scene Understanding and Generation
docs.text:
2026-05-01

HERMES++ is a unified driving world model that integrates 3D scene understanding and future geometry prediction within a single framework by leveraging a Bird’s-Eye View (BEV) representation and LLM-enhanced world queries.

docs.title: GR00T N1: An Open Foundation Model for Generalist Humanoid Robots
docs.text:
2026-05-01

GR00T N1 is an open Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation model for humanoid robots featuring a dual-system architecture that integrates a Vision-Language Model (System 2) for reasoning and a Diffusion Transformer (System 1) for real-time motor action generation.

docs.title: Generalizable Sparse-View 3D Reconstruction from Unconstrained Images
docs.text:
2026-05-01

GenWildSplat is a feed-forward framework that achieves generalizable 3D scene reconstruction from sparse, unposed images by integrating appearance adaptation and occlusion modeling without requiring per-scene optimization.

docs.title: Eureka: Human-Level Reward Design via Coding Large Language Models
docs.text:
2026-05-01

EUREKA is a novel reward design algorithm powered by coding Large Language Models (LLMs) that autonomously generates human-level reward functions for complex reinforcement learning tasks through evolutionary optimization.

docs.title: An Adaptable, Safe, and Portable Robot-Assisted Feeding System
docs.text:
2026-05-01

This paper presents an adaptable, safe, and portable robot-assisted feeding system designed to enable people with mobility impairments to feed themselves using multi-modal online learning for bite acquisition and real-time perception for bite transfer.

docs.title: An Adaptable, Safe, and Portable Robot-Assisted Feeding System
docs.text: Generated by JarvisForResearchers Bot on 2026-05-01

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text: We present a robot-assisted feeding system that integrates hardware (Kinova arms, Jetson Nano) with a ROS2 stack to enable self-feeding for mobility-impaired individuals. The system achieves adaptability to novel food textures via multi-modal online learning during bite acquisition and employs real-time, interaction-aware control for safe bite transfer into the mouth.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: A significant public health issue is the reliance on assistance for eating; in the United States alone, at least 1.8 million people require help with feeding. This dependency imposes substantial emotional burdens, specifically feelings of shame on the recipients, and creates a significant, time-intensive logistical burden on caregivers.

docs.title: Key Contributions
docs.text: Our work makes three primary contributions: 1. We deliver a robot-assisted feeding system that simultaneously embodies Safety, Portability, and User Control. 2. We employ multi-modal online learning to ensure tractable adaptation when encountering unseen food types during the bite acquisition phase. 3. We implement a bite transfer mechanism that utilizes real-time mouth perception coupled with an interaction-aware control strategy, leveraging multimodal sensing (visual and haptic).

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Figure 1: (Left) Diagram of the system logic. The user drives the app, which calls an API on the robot computer. (Right) Hardware system for both the Gen2 and Gen3 base. No external wires are needed. 1.2 Software Our software stack is built on ROS2 and the ros2-control frame- work2. We have configur

The system architecture integrates specific hardware components with a robust ROS2 software framework. User interaction is mediated through a React web application, which drives a finite state machine implemented via ROS2 action servers, services, and topics. Bite acquisition is managed using a 26-dimensional interpretable acquisition schema, augmented by a contextual bandit framework that incorporates haptic post hoc context for online adaptation. Bite transfer is achieved by combining real-time mouth perception derived from multiple in-hand cameras with a physical interaction-aware control method that fuses visual and haptic data to navigate within the user's oral cavity.

docs.title: Kinova Gen2 (JACO)
docs.text: This is a 6-DOF robot arm. It has been modified to incorporate an eye-in-hand system, which permits continuous rotation of the wrist joint, providing necessary degrees of freedom for precise manipulation tasks.

docs.title: Kinova Gen3
docs.text: This 6-DOF robot arm is equipped with an additional camera and features an Nvidia Jetson Nano developer kit mounted on its wrist. This setup allows for localized, high-throughput perception processing, specifically managing an Intel Realsense D415 RGBD camera.

docs.title: Custom, 3d-printed fork assembly
docs.text: This assembly is held by the robot gripper. Crucially, it is instrumented with a 6-DOF ATI Nano25 force-torque transducer, providing direct, high-fidelity feedback on contact forces during interaction.

docs.title: Lenovo Legion 5 laptop
docs.text: This unit serves as the primary computational hub. It is equipped with an Nvidia RTX 3060 6GB GPU and is mounted externally on the user's wheelchair, facilitating the system's portability requirement.

docs.title: React app
docs.text: This application functions as the primary user interface, accessible via a standard web browser. It acts as the logical control 'seat' for the entire system, managing high-level state transitions and user commands.

docs.title: Watchdog system
docs.text: This is a dedicated, single-node process responsible for enforcing hard safety constraints. It continuously verifies system invariants and issues an 'all-clear' signal, acting as a final layer of safety validation.

docs.title: Results
docs.text: No quantitative results were provided in the outline.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: The design explicitly addresses critical gaps in prior work. The system is engineered for portability, meaning it can operate using the battery power of any standard powered wheelchair without requiring dedicated, fixed wiring for the utensil. Furthermore, user agency is maintained because control is centralized in the web application, allowing the recipient to initiate, halt, or modify the system's operation at any point. Safety is not monolithic; it is enforced across four distinct layers: Compliant Hardware, Compliant Control, Software Anomaly Detection, and Emergency Intervention.

docs.title: Limitations & Open Questions
docs.text: Two primary limitations must be acknowledged. First, the system's usability is contingent on the user's capacity to interface with the web application using available assistive technologies. Second, the overall safety profile is fundamentally dependent on the operational integrity of the watchdog system and the reliability of the emergency stop mechanisms.

docs.text:

  author  = {Ethan Kroll Gordon and Rajat Kumar Jenamani and Amal Nanavati and Ziang Liu and Haya Bolotski and Raida Karim et al.},
  journal = {arXiv preprint arXiv:2403.04134},
